title: Microglossa ceylanica
taxonomy: Microglossa ceylanica is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Campanulaceae, commonly known as the bellflower family. This family comprises around 2,300 species of plants, many of which are annuals or perennials with bell-shaped flowers. Campanulaceae is further divided into several subfamilies and tribes, with Microglossa ceylanica belonging to the subfamily Lobelioideae. The plant is native to tropical regions, particularly in Sri Lanka and other parts of Asia. Further classification of Microglossa ceylanica may involve its classification into a specific genus and species within the Campanulaceae family.
uses: Microglossa ceylanica has been used in traditional medicine, particularly in Sri Lankan folk medicine. The plant is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. It is used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. Additionally, it is also used to treat skin conditions such as eczema and wounds. The exact mechanisms behind its medicinal properties are not yet fully understood.

intro: Microglossa ceylanica, also known as Ceylon vine or Ceylon bush, is a perennial climber native to the tropical forests of Sri Lanka and India. This plant has been traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin disorders, due to its antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Microglossa ceylanica include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Herbal preparations made from Microglossa ceylanica include decoctions, infusions, and ointments, which are used to treat fever, skin conditions, and joint pain.
